1. Click [10,32] with both blocks stamped: board_changed=False, gameplay_changed=False (no effect!)

So clicking [10,32] had no effect this time, but clicking [34,32] did have an effect. This is different from before when [10,32] had an effect.

1. Initial state: both unstamped
2. Toggle red: red stamped
3. Toggle blue: blue stamped
4. Click [10,32]: no effect (board_changed=False)
5. Click [34,32]: gameplay changed
So [10,32] had no effect when both blocks were stamped, but it had an effect earlier when both blocks were unstamped. This suggests that the effect of clicking divider squares depends on the block configuration.
Let me think about this more carefully:
- When blocks are unstamped, clicking [10,32] causes a gameplay change (shrinks white bar)
- When blocks are stamped, clicking [10,32] has no effect
- When blocks are stamped, clicking [34,32] causes a gameplay change
This suggests that the divider squares are "conditional" - they only work when the blocks are in a specific configuration.

- [34,32] works when blocks are stamped

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test all divider squares to find interactive ones
# Divider squares at rows: 10, 14, 18, 22, 26, 30, 34, 38, 42, 46, 50
# Current state: both blocks stamped

# First check white bar
seg = current_frame.segmentation
white_bar_size = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 10:
        b = n['boundary']
        r1 = min(p[0] for p in b)
        if r1 >= 60:
            white_bar_size = n['pixels']
            break
print(f"White bar size: {white_bar_size}")

# Test each divider square
divider_rows = [10, 14, 18, 22, 26, 30, 34, 38, 42, 46, 50]
interactive = []
for row in divider_rows:
    result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': row, 'col': 32}])
    bc = last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)
    gc = last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed', False)
    if gc:
        interactive.append(row)
        # Check white bar
        seg = current_frame.segmentation
        for n in seg['nodes']:
            if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 10:
                b = n['boundary']
                r1 = min(p[0] for p in b)
                if r1 >= 60:
                    print(f"  Row {row}: interactive, white bar now {n['pixels']}")
                    white_bar_size = n['pixels']
                    break
    else:
        print(f"  Row {row}: no effect")
    if last_action_result.get('level_completed'):
        print("LEVEL COMPLETED!")
        break

print(f"\nInteractive divider rows (both stamped): {interactive}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
